regulator: VARA (Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority -- Emirate of Dubai, including its free zones, excluding the DIFC) / CMA (Capital Markets Authority -- federal, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM; legal successor to the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) since 1 January 2026) / DFSA (Dubai Financial Services Authority -- DIFC only) / FSRA (Financial Services Regulatory Authority -- ADGM only)
instruments: Virtual Assets and Related Activities Regulations 2023 -- under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC); confirmed by direct fetch of VARA's own rulebook portal, rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/virtual-assets-and-related-activities-regulations-2023, which states the current consolidated version is effective 19 June 2025, with the regulations originally published 7 February 2023; the ten-part structure runs from Part I (establishing VARA) through Part IV (licensing/authorisation) and Part V (VA Activity Rulebooks, including the Exchange Services rulebook) to Part VIII (market offences/insider dealing) and Part IX (supervision/enforcement)

VARA Exchange Services Rulebook -- under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC); confirmed by direct fetch of rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/exchange-services-rulebook, which shows the rulebook's current version effective 31 March 2026 (an intermediate version was issued 19 May 2025; the rulebook originates from 7 February 2023); it governs entities holding VARA's exchange services licence and covers trading-venue operation and participant conduct, margin trading (prudential/margin-sum requirements), exchange-traded derivatives services, market surveillance, trading-system continuity and settlement

Federal Decree-Law No. (32) of 2025 Concerning the Capital Market Authority -- under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM); confirmed by direct fetch of the CMA's own domain, uaecma.gov.ae/en/new-cma-law, which states the decree-law entered into effect 1 January 2026, repealing Federal Law No. 4 of 2000 Concerning the Emirates Securities and Commodities Authority and Market in its entirety and reconstituting the former Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) as the Capital Market Authority (CMA), an independent federal public authority with its own legal personality that succeeds the SCA in all its rights, obligations and contracts; the full decree-law text is hosted directly on uaecma.gov.ae (assets/download/.../federal-decree-law-no-32-of-2025-en-capital-market-authority.aspx)

Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025 Concerning the Regulation of the Capital Market -- under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM); confirmed via the same uaecma.gov.ae/en/new-cma-law page (direct fetch), effective 1 January 2026 alongside Decree-Law No. 32/2025; it recodifies the federal licensing regime for regulated financial activities, offerings of securities and market conduct, and expands the scope of federal 'financial products'/regulated activities to include virtual assets -- the enabling federal statute under which the CMA's virtual-asset activity licensing decisions (below) were subsequently issued

CMA Virtual Assets Framework (five core modules: General Requirements, Conduct of Business, Alternative Trading System, Anti-Money Laundering/Counter-Terrorist Financing, and Prudential Requirements; eight licensed virtual-asset activities, up from three under the prior SCA regime) -- under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM); the CMA's own domain, uaecma.gov.ae (home-page news listing, directly fetched this session), carries the CMA's own headline announcement dated 13 April 2026: 'The CMA Issues the Virtual Assets Framework, Establishing an Integrated Regulatory Regime Comprising Five Core Modules and Eight Regulated Activities', with an on-record quote from CMA CEO Waleed Saeed Al Awadhi; the framework replaces the CMA/SCA's prior, narrower three-activity virtual-asset regime referenced on the UAE's federal portal u.ae (which, as fetched this session, still describes the pre-2026 state made under Cabinet Resolution No. 111 of 2022) -- corroborating secondary legal-alert sources (Clyde & Co, Horizons & Co, Dentons) consistently identify the operative instrument as CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026, said to have been issued 13 February 2026, but this exact decision number and issuance date were not independently confirmed by direct fetch of the CMA's own decision text this session and are flagged in open_items accordingly

DFSA Crypto Token regime -- under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC; confirmed by direct fetch of the DFSA Rulebook's own hosted page, dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/1-november-2022-dfsa-crypto-token-regime-comes-force, which states the regime came into force 1 November 2022 by extending existing DFSA financial-service-activity modules (advising, dealing, arranging, and custody, among others) to Crypto Tokens, and covers AML/CFT, consumer protection, market integrity, custody and financial-resources risk

DFSA updated Crypto Token rules (in force 12 January 2026) -- under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC; the update itself was confirmed this session by direct fetch of the DFSA's own PDF media release hosted on dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com (file metadata confirms a creation date of 12 January 2026, title referencing updated Crypto Token rules), corroborated by independent secondary legal-alert reporting (Clyde & Co, Simmons & Simmons, Dechert); the central change is a shift from a DFSA-maintained 'Recognised Crypto Token' list to firm-led, self-assessed suitability determinations made on a reasoned and documented basis against DFSA suitability criteria, with a transition period during which tokens previously on the discontinued Recognised Crypto Token list are automatically treated as suitable; Fiat Crypto Tokens remain an exception -- the DFSA itself continues to assess and approve these directly rather than delegating that assessment to firms

FSRA Crypto Asset Legislative Framework (Regulation of Crypto Asset Activities in ADGM) -- under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM; confirmed by direct fetch of adgm.com's own announcement, adgm.com/media/announcements/adgm-launches-crypto-asset-regulatory-framework, which dates the launch to 25 June 2018 and states the framework covers spot crypto-asset activities carried on by crypto-asset exchanges, custodians and other intermediaries in ADGM, addressing money-laundering/financial-crime, consumer-protection, technology-governance, custody and exchange-operation risk; the original framework also introduced a Daily Value Trading Levy on crypto exchanges on a sliding scale

FSRA 2025 amendments to the Digital Asset Regulatory Framework (following Consultation Paper No. 11 of 2024) -- under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM; confirmed by direct fetch of adgm.com's own announcement, adgm.com/media/announcements/adgm-fsra-implements-amendments-to-its-digital-asset-regulatory-framework, dated 10 June 2025 and effective immediately; it replaces the FSRA's approval-based process for 'Accepted Virtual Assets' (AVAs -- the only virtual assets ADGM-licensed firms may use for regulated activity, including exchange/trading) with a notification-based process resting on the Authorised Person's own self-assessment against FSRA-defined criteria, requires a public list of AVAs used by each firm, formally prohibits privacy tokens and algorithmic stablecoins from use in ADGM-regulated financial services, adjusts capital requirements/fees for VA firms, introduces a VA-specific product intervention power, and expands permitted investment scope for Venture Capital Funds
standing_summary: The UAE has no single national licensing regime for virtual-asset exchanges and trading platforms; instead, four separate regulators license and supervise exchange/VASP activity, each within its own distinct geography, and none of their rules may be read as a general 'UAE rule' for this pillar. First, under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC), the Virtual Assets and Related Activities Regulations 2023 (current consolidated version effective 19 June 2025, confirmed by direct fetch of rulebooks.vara.ae) require licensing to conduct VA Activities, with exchange-specific conduct set out in VARA's Exchange Services Rulebook (current version effective 31 March 2026, also directly confirmed), covering trading-venue operation, margin trading, exchange-traded derivatives services, market surveillance and settlement. Second, under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM), the Capital Markets Authority -- reconstituted from the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) effective 1 January 2026 by Federal Decree-Laws No. 32 and No. 33 of 2025 (both confirmed by direct fetch of uaecma.gov.ae, the CMA's own domain) -- issued a Virtual Assets Framework, announced on the CMA's own site on 13 April 2026, built on five core modules (General Requirements, Conduct of Business, Alternative Trading System, AML/CTF, and Prudential Requirements) and expanding licensed virtual-asset activities from three to eight, including dealing in virtual assets as principal or agent, providing or arranging custody, operating a multilateral/alternative trading system, investment advice, portfolio management and arranging investment deals; consistent secondary legal-alert reporting identifies the operative instrument as CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026 (said to be issued 13 February 2026, replacing the SCA's narrower prior three-activity virtual-asset regime) and reports minimum capital requirements tiered from roughly AED 500,000 to AED 4 million by activity, plus prohibitions on privacy tokens and algorithmic tokens and restrictions on utility tokens/NFTs -- but this card treats the precise decision number, issuance date, and capital-requirement figures as corroborated-but-not-independently-primary-verified, since a direct fetch of the CMA's own decision text was not achieved this session (see open_items). Third, under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC, the Crypto Token regime came into force 1 November 2022 (confirmed by direct fetch of the DFSA's own rulebook host, dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com) by extending existing DFSA financial-service-activity modules to Crypto Tokens; an updated regime came into force 12 January 2026 (DFSA's own hosted PDF media release, directly fetched, confirms the 12 January 2026 date via file metadata, corroborated by independent secondary reporting), replacing the DFSA-maintained Recognised Crypto Token list with firm-led, documented self-assessment of token suitability against DFSA criteria -- Fiat Crypto Tokens remain an exception the DFSA continues to assess directly. Fourth, under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM, the Financial Services Regulatory Authority launched the Gulf region's first dedicated crypto-asset framework on 25 June 2018 (confirmed by direct fetch of adgm.com), covering spot crypto-asset exchanges, custodians and intermediaries; 2025 amendments (in force 10 June 2025, following Consultation Paper No. 11 of 2024, also confirmed by direct fetch of adgm.com) moved the 'Accepted Virtual Assets' gateway that ADGM-licensed exchanges and other firms must use from FSRA pre-approval to firm self-assessment with mandatory notification, formally prohibited privacy tokens and algorithmic stablecoins from ADGM-regulated financial services, and adjusted capital/fee requirements and product-intervention powers. Across all four regimes, none may be described as a national UAE rule: VARA's licence does not authorise DIFC or ADGM activity or extend federally; the CMA's federal framework expressly excludes the DIFC and ADGM; DFSA authorisation is DIFC-only; FSRA authorisation is ADGM-only. This card does not include Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE) instruments, which the pillar brief allocates to the Stablecoins and Banking & Money pillars rather than to exchange/VATP licensing.

u.ae (UAE federal government portal): Regulation of digital properties overview page (fetched this session; reflects pre-13-April-2026 CMA framework state, retained here as corroboration of the framework the CMA's Virtual Assets Framework replaced)

open_items: The CMA's own Virtual Assets Framework announcement (uaecma.gov.ae, 13 April 2026, directly fetched) confirms the five-module/eight-activity structure and CEO quote, but this session could not directly fetch the CMA's own decision text to independently confirm the specific instrument number and date reported consistently by secondary legal-alert sources as 'CMA Decision No. 4/R.M/2026', issued 13 February 2026. Both uaecma.gov.ae's regulations-listing page (fetched this session) and a direct fetch attempt at the decision itself did not surface the decision document; the number/date should be re-verified against a primary CMA document (e.g. the Official Gazette or uaecma.gov.ae's own regulations archive) before being asserted as fact rather than as reported.

Reported minimum capital requirements for CMA-licensed virtual-asset activities (secondary sources describe a range from roughly AED 500,000 to AED 4,000,000 depending on activity and whether client assets are held) and the CMA's reported phased implementation timeline (pilot ~July 2026; larger firms ~January 2027; smaller firms ~July 2027; government entities ~October 2027) were not independently confirmed via a direct fetch of CMA primary text this session and are omitted from the standing summary and instruments list pending verification.

This card treats VARA, the CMA, the DFSA and the FSRA as the four regulators relevant to exchange/VASP licensing per the pillar brief; it does not cover the Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE), whose Payment Token Services Regulation and banking-side rules are allocated to the Stablecoins and Banking & Money pillars respectively, even though CBUAE coordination is referenced in general terms on u.ae.

dfsa.ae (the DFSA's primary corporate domain) returned HTTP 403 to direct fetch attempts this session; DFSA facts in this card are instead sourced from direct fetches of dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com, the DFSA's own Thomson Reuters-hosted Rulebook platform (the same platform pattern the pillar brief explicitly treats as FSRA's own domain for ADGM, i.e. en.adgm.thomsonreuters.com), plus file metadata from a DFSA-branded PDF hosted on that same platform. This should be re-attempted against www.dfsa.ae directly in a future session in case the 403 was a transient fetch-tooling issue rather than a durable block.

uaecma.gov.ae's own 'Latest Regulations' listing page, fetched this session, showed only three unrelated Chairman-level decisions from 2026 and did not surface the Virtual Assets Framework/Decision 4/R.M/2026 in that particular listing -- consistent with the framework having been issued as a Board (R.M.) rather than Chairman-level decision, but this distinction has not been independently confirmed and is flagged for a future session with direct access to the CMA's full regulations archive.

VARA's licence categories beyond 'Exchange Services' (e.g. broker-dealer, custody, and the compulsory rulebooks referenced in Part V of the Virtual Assets and Related Activities Regulations 2023) were only partially enumerated by this session's VARA rulebook fetches and are tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar rather than duplicated here.
